1: import os
2: import glob
3: import geopandas as gpd
4: 
5: def clean_shapefiles():
6:     raw_dir = "/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/nga_shp"
7:     clean_dir = "/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/clean/nga_shp"
8:     os.makedirs(clean_dir, exist_ok=True)
9:     
10:     bay_states = ["Adamawa", "Borno", "Yobe"]
11:     
12:     # Load Admin 1 to create the BAY states union geometry for spatial operations
13:     admin1_path = os.path.join(raw_dir, "nga_admin1.shp")
14:     if not os.path.exists(admin1_path):
15:         print(f"Error: Admin 1 shapefile not found at {admin1_path}")
16:         return
17:         
18:     admin1_df = gpd.read_file(admin1_path)
19:     bay_admin1 = admin1_df[admin1_df["adm1_name"].isin(bay_states)]
20:     
21:     # Use union_all() if available, fallback to unary_union
22:     if hasattr(bay_admin1.geometry, "union_all"):
23:         bay_union = bay_admin1.geometry.union_all()
24:     else:
25:         bay_union = bay_admin1.geometry.unary_union
26:         
27:     shp_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(raw_dir, "*.shp"))
28:     
29:     for shp_path in sorted(shp_files):
30:         base = os.path.basename(shp_path)
31:         name, ext = os.path.splitext(base)
32:         print(f"\n--- Processing {base} ---")
33:         
34:         df = gpd.read_file(shp_path)
35:         print(f"Original shape: {df.shape}")
36:         
37:         filtered_df = None
38:         
39:         # Determine filtering strategy based on filename and columns
40:         if "admin1" in name:
41:             filtered_df = df[df["adm1_name"].isin(bay_states)]
42:         elif "admin2" in name:
43:             filtered_df = df[df["adm1_name"].isin(bay_states)]
44:         elif "admin3" in name:
45:             filtered_df = df[df["adm1_name"].isin(bay_states)]
46:         elif "admincapitals" in name:
47:             filtered_df = df[df["adm1_name"].isin(bay_states)]
48:         elif "adminpoints" in name:
49:             filtered_df = df[df["adm1_name"].isin(bay_states)]
50:         elif "senatorialdistricts" in name:
51:             if "adm1_en" in df.columns:
52:                 filtered_df = df[df["adm1_en"].isin(bay_states)]
53:             elif "admin1name" in df.columns:
54:                 filtered_df = df[df["admin1name"].isin(bay_states)]
55:         elif "adminlines" in name:
56:             # Clip spatially and keep only line geometry types to avoid mixed types
57:             clipped = gpd.clip(df, bay_union)
58:             filtered_df = clipped[clipped.geometry.type.isin(["LineString", "MultiLineString"])]
59:         elif "admin0" in name:
60:             # Clip spatially and keep only polygon geometry types
61:             clipped = gpd.clip(df, bay_union)
62:             filtered_df = clipped[clipped.geometry.type.isin(["Polygon", "MultiPolygon"])]
63:             # Update attributes to reflect the BAY region
64:             for col in ["adm0_name", "adm0_name1", "adm0_name2", "adm0_name3", "adm0_en", "admin0name"]:
65:                 if col in filtered_df.columns:
66:                     filtered_df[col] = "BAY States"
67:         else:
68:             # Fallback based on columns
69:             state_cols = ["adm1_name", "adm1_en", "admin1name", "state"]
70:             found_col = None
71:             for col in state_cols:
72:                 if col in df.columns:
73:                     found_col = col
74:                     break
75:             
76:             if found_col:
77:                 filtered_df = df[df[found_col].isin(bay_states)]
78:             else:
79:                 # If no state column found, clip spatially and keep geom type of original first element
80:                 clipped = gpd.clip(df, bay_union)
81:                 orig_geom_type = df.geometry.iloc[0].geom_type if not df.empty else ""
82:                 if "Polygon" in orig_geom_type or "MultiPolygon" in orig_geom_type:
83:                     filtered_df = clipped[clipped.geometry.type.isin(["Polygon", "MultiPolygon"])]
84:                 elif "LineString" in orig_geom_type or "MultiLineString" in orig_geom_type:
85:                     filtered_df = clipped[clipped.geometry.type.isin(["LineString", "MultiLineString"])]
86:                 elif "Point" in orig_geom_type or "MultiPoint" in orig_geom_type:
87:                     filtered_df = clipped[clipped.geometry.type.isin(["Point", "MultiPoint"])]
88:                 else:
89:                     filtered_df = clipped
90:                 
91:         if filtered_df is not None and not filtered_df.empty:
92:             out_path = os.path.join(clean_dir, base)
93:             filtered_df.to_file(out_path)
94:             print(f"Saved to {out_path}")
95:             print(f"New shape: {filtered_df.shape}")
96:         else:
97:             print("Filtered result is empty. Skipping.")
98: 
99: if __name__ == "__main__":
100:     clean_shapefiles()
101:

The search for "world population data sources Nigeria" returned the following summary:
To access reliable population data for Nigeria, you can utilize the following official government and international sources:

### Official Government Sources
*   **National Population Commission (NPC):** This is the primary government agency responsible for collecting, analyzing, and publishing demographic data in Nigeria[1][2]. They manage national censuses, civil registration, and vital statistics (births, deaths, etc.)[3][4].
    *   **Website:** [nationalpopulation.gov.ng](https://nationalpopulation.gov.ng)[1]
*   **National Bureau of Statistics (NBS):** The NBS is the central body for official socio-economic statistics in Nigeria[5][6]. Their data portal provides comprehensive statistical information, including key indicators for population and development[7][6].
    *   **Website:** [nigerianstat.gov.ng](https://nigerianstat.gov.ng)[6]

### International Organizations
These organizations frequently compile and analyze data from national statistical offices to provide global comparisons and projections:

*   **United Nations Population Division (UN DESA):** Their *World Population Prospects* is one of the most widely cited sources for population estimates and projections[8][9].
    *   **Data Portal:** [population.un.org](https://population.un.org)[8]
*   **United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA):** The UNFPA focuses on reproductive health and population data, often providing dashboards and reports on Nigeria’s demographic trends[10][11].
    *   **Website:** [unfpa.org](https://www.unfpa.org)
*   **World Bank Open Data:** The World Bank aggregates data from official national and international sources, offering historical time series for Nigeria's total population and other demographic indicators[8][12].
    *   **Website:** [data.worldbank.org](https://data.worldbank.org)
*   **Our World in Data:** This platform provides user-friendly visualizations and analysis of Nigeria's demographic history and trends, drawing primarily from the UN’s *World Population Prospects*[13][14].
    *   **Website:** [ourworldindata.org](https://ourworldindata.org)[14]
*   **Global Health Data Exchange (GHDx):** Useful for finding specific datasets related to Nigeria's health and census surveys, including historical records[15].
    *   **Website:** [ghdx.healthdata.org](https://ghdx.healthdata.org)[15]

When using these sources, it is best practice to check the "Methodology" or "Data Source" notes provided by each platform to understand whether the figures are based on actual census counts, administrative registers, or statistical projections[13][14].

The search for "GRID3 WorldPop HDX population data Nigeria" returned the following summary:
The **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)** platform and the **GRID3 Data Hub** provide access to high-resolution, gridded population data for Nigeria[1][2][3].

### Key Resources for Nigeria Population Data

*   **GRID3 Data Hub:** This is the primary repository for GRID3-related data products[1]. It offers tools to visualize, analyze, and download data on population, settlements, administrative boundaries, and infrastructure[1][3].
    *   **Website:** [GRID3 Data Hub](https://grid3.org/data-hub)[1][3]
*   **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX):** You can search the HDX platform for datasets tagged with "GRID3" and "Nigeria"[2]. This includes various datasets such as population estimates, settlement extents, and operational ward boundaries[2][4].
    *   **Website:** [HDX - GRID3 Nigeria Datasets](https://data.humdata.org/)[2]

### About the Data
*   **Resolution:** Much of the population data provided by GRID3 and WorldPop is available at a high spatial resolution of approximately **100-meter grid cells** (3 arc-seconds)[5][6][3].
*   **Content:** These datasets often include gridded population estimates, demographics (age and sex groups), and are frequently aligned with GRID3 settlement extents to provide a detailed view of population distribution[5][7][8].
*   **Recent Releases:** As of mid-2026, updated population estimates and settlement data continue to be published to support humanitarian and development decision-making[7][3][8]. For example, "Nigeria Population Estimates v3.0" was released in August 2025[3].

If you have specific questions about a dataset or encounter issues with access, you can typically find contact information for the data curators (such as WorldPop or CIESIN) directly on the respective dataset pages on HDX or the GRID3 Data Hub[6][2][3].

The search for "GPW Landscan HRSL population Nigeria" returned the following summary:
For information regarding gridded population datasets in Nigeria, including **GPW** (Gridded Population of the World), **LandScan**, and **HRSL** (High Resolution Settlement Layer), please refer to the following summary:

### **Overview of Datasets in Nigeria**
These datasets are commonly used for humanitarian, development, and research purposes in Nigeria to estimate population distribution when census data is older or aggregated at coarse administrative levels[1][2].

*   **GPW (Gridded Population of the World, v4.11):** Produced by CIESIN at Columbia University, this is a "minimally modeled" dataset[1][3]. It distributes census counts (typically from 2006 Local Government Area data in Nigeria) uniformly across grid cells[1][4]. It is useful for global consistency but does not account for the actual spatial distribution of settlements within those areas[1][4].
*   **LandScan:** Developed by Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), this dataset uses a different approach, distributing national population estimates based on land cover and other geospatial indicators to provide an "ambient" population measure at approximately 1 km resolution (though newer High Definition/HD versions exist at higher resolutions)[5][6][7].
*   **HRSL (High Resolution Settlement Layer):** This dataset provides population estimates at a very high resolution (approximately 30m)[8]. It is often praised for its ability to better delineate settlements—including in urban and rural areas—by identifying the presence of buildings using high-resolution satellite imagery[8][2].

### **Availability and Resources**
*   **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX):** You can find various population datasets for Nigeria, including high-resolution density maps and demographic estimates, on the [HDX platform](https://data.humdata.org/)[9][10].
*   **GRID3 (Geo-Referenced Infrastructure and Demographic Data for Development):** This program provides comprehensive geospatial data for Nigeria, including updated population estimates (e.g., NGA Population v3.0, released in 2025), which often combine settlement footprints with official census and survey data[11][12].
*   **WorldPop:** Frequently used in conjunction with or as an alternative to the above, WorldPop provides detailed, model-based population estimates for Nigeria using machine learning and geospatial covariates[13][14][15].

### **Comparison and Accuracy**
Research comparing these datasets in Nigerian contexts (such as in Lagos or Port Harcourt) has noted that:
*   All gridded population datasets may struggle to accurately capture populations in informal urban settlements (slums), often underestimating them[16][17].
*   **HRSL** has been noted in some studies as providing relatively better estimates in specific urban contexts compared to some coarser gridded products[16][17][2].
*   The choice of dataset often depends on your specific application; for example, if you need to model dynamic population movement, **LandScan** may be preferred, whereas for detailed settlement mapping, **HRSL** or **WorldPop/GRID3** products are frequently utilized[5][12][7].

[2026-06-26T22:38:28Z] [STEP 22] AGENT ACTION:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Response:
The following is a comprehensive summary of the primary national, international, and high-resolution gridded sources available for world population data for Nigeria.

---

### 1. National & Official Government Sources
*   **National Population Commission (NPC):** The primary government agency responsible for national censuses, civil registration, and demographic surveys (e.g., Demographic and Health Surveys - DHS).
    *   **Website:** [nationalpopulation.gov.ng](https://nationalpopulation.gov.ng)
*   **National Bureau of Statistics (NBS):** The central coordinator for all official socio-economic statistics in Nigeria. They publish reports, indices, and demographic indicators.
    *   **Website:** [nigerianstat.gov.ng](https://nigerianstat.gov.ng)

---

### 2. High-Resolution & Gridded Population Projects (Geospatial)
*   **GRID3 (Geo-Referenced Infrastructure and Demographic Data for Development):** Highly detailed demographic data, settlement footprints, and administrative boundaries. The *GRID3 Nigeria Population Estimates (v3.0, released in 2025)* provides model-based population estimates at a ~100m grid cell resolution.
    *   **Website:** [grid3.org](https://grid3.org/data-hub)
*   **WorldPop:** Produced by the University of Southampton, WorldPop uses machine learning and spatial covariates to distribute population estimates into 100m grid cells.
    *   **Website:** [worldpop.org](https://www.worldpop.org)
*   **Meta's High-Resolution Settlement Layer (HRSL):** Developed in collaboration with CIESIN (Columbia University), the HRSL uses computer vision on high-resolution satellite imagery to detect building footprints and distribute population at a ~30m resolution.
    *   **Website:** Available via the [Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)](https://data.humdata.org/)
*   **LandScan (Oak Ridge National Laboratory):** Provides a 1km resolution database representing the "ambient" (average/dynamic) population distribution rather than static household locations.
    *   **Website:** [landscan.ornl.gov](https://landscan.ornl.gov)
*   **CIESIN GPW (Gridded Population of the World):** A minimally modeled dataset that simply distributes census counts uniformly across administrative units.
    *   **Website:** [sedac.ciesin.columbia.edu](https://sedac.ciesin.columbia.edu)

---

### 3. Global Aggregators & Demographic Projections
*   **United Nations Population Division (UN DESA):** Publishes the *World Population Prospects*, which is the benchmark source for global population estimates and projections.
    *   **Website:** [population.un.org](https://population.un.org)
*   **World Bank Open Data:** Aggregates official country estimates alongside international agency statistics to offer historical time-series data for total, urban, and rural population growth.
    *   **Website:** [data.worldbank.org](https://data.worldbank.org)
*   **Our World in Data:** Provides interactive visualizations of historical demographics and future projections based on UN and World Bank datasets.
    *   **Website:** [ourworldindata.org](https://ourworldindata.org)

---

### 4. Displacement & Humanitarian Specific Sources
*   **IOM Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM):** Tracks internally displaced populations (IDPs) and returnees, especially in conflict-affected regions like northeastern Nigeria. 
    *   *Note:* A local copy of this dataset for Round 50 site assessments in the BAY region (Borno, Adamawa, Yobe) is located in your workspace at [DTM Nigeria Site Assessment Round 50.xlsx](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/DTM%20Nigeria%20Site%20Assessment%20Round%2050.xlsx).
*   **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX):** Managed by OCHA, HDX hosts aggregated geospatial and population datasets curated specifically for humanitarian actions in Nigeria.
    *   **Website:** [data.humdata.org](https://data.humdata.org)

---

### Summary of Work Done
*   Listed official governmental statistical bodies for Nigeria (**NPC** and **NBS**).
*   Categorized gridded, high-resolution geospatial datasets (**GRID3**, **WorldPop**, **HRSL**, **LandScan**, **GPW**).
*   Linked standard international sources for aggregated estimates (**UN DESA**, **World Bank**, **Our World in Data**).
*   Highlighted displacement-specific sources, including a direct link to the local [DTM Nigeria Site Assessment Round 50.xlsx](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/DTM%20Nigeria%20Site%20Assessment%20Round%2050.xlsx) spreadsheet within the project workspace.

[2026-06-26T22:39:40Z] [STEP 25] TOOL OUTPUT (SEARCH_WEB):
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Created At: 2026-06-26T22:39:40Z
Completed At: 2026-06-26T22:39:45Z
The search for "open source population dataset download formats Nigeria GRID3 WorldPop HDX" returned the following summary:
Nigeria population datasets produced by **GRID3** (Geo-Referenced Infrastructure and Demographic Data for Development) and **WorldPop** are widely available as open-access resources[1][2][3]. These datasets are frequently hosted on the **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)**, the GRID3 Data Hub, and the WorldPop portal[4][5][6][7].

### Common Download Formats
Depending on the specific dataset and platform, population data for Nigeria is typically available in the following formats:

*   **Geospatial Raster Formats:**
    *   **GeoTIFF (.tif):** The standard format for gridded population estimates (e.g., 100m or 1km resolution)[8][9][10].
    *   **ASCII XYZ:** Often provided as an alternative raster format for gridded data[6][11].
*   **Vector and Tabular Formats:**
    *   **Shapefile (.shp):** Commonly used for administrative boundaries (e.g., States, LGAs, Wards) when population data is summarized by area[5][12][13].
    *   **Geopackage (.gpkg):** Increasingly available for administrative boundaries and related geographic data[5].
    *   **CSV/Spreadsheets (.csv, .xlsx):** Used for non-spatial or summarized demographic statistics (e.g., population totals for administrative units)[12][14].
*   **Compressed Formats:**
    *   **ZIP (.zip):** Datasets are almost always provided as compressed archives containing the primary data files along with metadata and documentation[12][15].

### Where to Access the Data
*   **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX):** Search for "Nigeria population" on [data.humdata.org](https://data.humdata.org/) to find aggregated datasets, often including links to both gridded (raster) and tabular (CSV/Shapefile) data[5][16][7].
*   **GRID3 Data Hub:** The [GRID3 Nigeria Data Portal](https://grid3.gov.ng/) or the main [GRID3 Data Hub](https://grid3.org/) provides access to high-resolution population estimates, settlement extents, and operational ward boundaries[4][17][12].
*   **WorldPop Open Population Repository (WOPR):** Offers high-resolution "bottom-up" and "top-down" gridded estimates[6][3]. You can also use the **wopr R package** for programmatic access to these datasets[18].

### Important Notes
*   **Licensing:** Most of these datasets are released under **Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) licenses**, allowing for open use provided the source is properly cited[15][19][20].
*   **Resolution:** Gridded data is generally available at **3 arc-seconds (~100m)** or **30 arc-seconds (~1km)** resolutions[21][9][6].
*   **Methodology:** Always check the documentation accompanying the download (e.g., whether the data is "constrained" or "unconstrained") to ensure it meets your specific research or operational needs[6][22].

[2026-06-26T22:39:45Z] [STEP 26] AGENT ACTION:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Response:
Several open-source and open-access population datasets for Nigeria are available for direct download. These datasets are distributed under open licenses—most commonly **Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY 4.0)**—allowing you to use, adapt, and share the data.

Below are the key platforms, what they offer, and the formats available for download:

---

### 1. GRID3 Nigeria Data Portal
GRID3 offers high-resolution, bottom-up population estimates modeled using building footprints and survey data.
*   **What is downloadable:** 
    *   Gridded population estimates (by total and demographic age/sex cohorts).
    *   Settlement extents (delineation of built-up areas).
    *   Operational Ward Boundaries (GIS vectors).
*   **Download Formats:** **GeoTIFF (.tif)** for rasters; **Shapefiles (.shp)** and **GeoPackage (.gpkg)** for vectors; **CSV** for tabular data.
*   **Access:** [GRID3 Nigeria Hub](https://grid3.org/data-hub) or [grid3.gov.ng](https://grid3.gov.ng/)

### 2. WorldPop Open Data Repository
WorldPop provides open-access, high-resolution (100m) gridded population estimates derived from statistical models.
*   **What is downloadable:**
    *   Constrained and unconstrained gridded population counts.
    *   Demographic age/sex structures.
    *   Annual time-series population estimates.
*   **Download Formats:** **GeoTIFF (.tif)**, **ASCII XYZ**, and **CSV**.
*   **Programmatic Access:** Programmatic download is supported via Python APIs and the `wopr` R package.
*   **Access:** [WorldPop Open Population Repository](https://www.worldpop.org)

### 3. Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)
HDX is a repository managed by OCHA that compiles datasets from various humanitarian partners working in Nigeria.
*   **What is downloadable:** 
    *   Meta's High Resolution Settlement Layer (HRSL) population density (30m resolution).
    *   GRID3 operational and administrative boundaries.
    *   Displaced populations and survey aggregates.
*   **Download Formats:** **ZIP** archives containing **Shapefiles (.shp)**, **GeoJSON**, **GeoTIFF (.tif)**, and **CSV / Excel (.xlsx)**.
*   **Access:** [HDX Nigeria Datasets](https://data.humdata.org/group/nga)

### 4. UN DESA (World Population Prospects)
For macro-demographic statistics and long-term population projections (national-level).
*   **What is downloadable:**
    *   Historical and projected population numbers (up to 2100).
    *   Fertility, mortality, and migration datasets.
*   **Download Formats:** **Excel (.xlsx)** spreadsheets and flat **CSV** files.
*   **Access:** [UN DESA WPP Download Portal](https://population.un.org/wpp/Download/)

---

### Local Resources in Your Workspace
If you need immediate local access to open-source population/geospatial datasets, your current workspace contains:
*   **Displacement Population Data:** [DTM Nigeria Site Assessment Round 50.xlsx](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/DTM%20Nigeria%20Site%20Assessment%20Round%2050.xlsx) (Tabular Excel format containing displacement site demographic estimates).
*   **Administrative Boundaries:** Open-source shapefiles located in the [nga_shp](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/nga_shp/) directory (Shapefile format ready for geospatial mapping).

---

### Summary of Work Done
*   Identified the specific open-source download portals for Nigerian population data (**GRID3**, **WorldPop**, **HDX**, and **UN DESA**).
*   Listed the available file download formats (**GeoTIFF**, **Shapefile**, **GeoPackage**, **CSV**, **Excel**).
*   Detailed the licensing details (**CC BY**) and download options.
*   Linked local open-source resources in the current workspace including the [DTM assessment dataset](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/DTM%20Nigeria%20Site%20Assessment%20Round%2050.xlsx) and the [GIS boundaries folder](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/nga_shp/).

Created At: 2026-06-26T22:41:35Z

The search for "Nigeria LGA population dataset download site:data.humdata.org" returned the following summary:
You can access Nigeria Local Government Area (LGA) population datasets on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) website. Below are the most relevant datasets and direct paths to find them:

### Recommended Datasets
*   **[Nigeria - Subnational Population Statistics](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ab-nga)**: This is the primary resource for subnational population data. It provides statistics projected from the 2006 census, disaggregated by administrative levels (including LGAs), which are designed to be linked with Nigeria's administrative boundary shapefiles[1].
*   **[Bottom-up Gridded Population Estimates for Nigeria](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nga-population-v2-0-admin)**: This dataset contains population totals for states and LGAs. It also includes gridded population estimates summarized by LGA boundaries, available as both shapefiles (with population in the attribute tables) and CSV spreadsheets[2].
*   **[Nigeria - Population Density](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nigeria-population-density)**: If your work requires gridded density data rather than administrative totals, WorldPop and CIESIN provide high-resolution population density datasets for Nigeria on HDX[3].

### Tips for Searching HDX
To find these and other related files, you can use the search bar on [data.humdata.org](https://data.humdata.org) with the following terms:
*   `Nigeria population LGA`
*   `Nigeria subnational administrative boundaries`
*   `GRID3 Nigeria` (often contains high-resolution, operational-level population and ward data)[2][4]

**Data Format Note:** Most of these datasets are available in multiple formats, including **CSV, XLSX, SHP (Shapefile), and Geodatabase**, making them compatible with most GIS and data analysis software[2][5][6]. Always check the "Caveats" section on the specific dataset page for information regarding the reference year and methodology used for the population projections[1][5].

The search for "site:data.humdata.org/dataset/ bottom-up gridded population estimates for nigeria csv" returned the following summary:
You can find the "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria" datasets on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) website. These datasets are available in multiple versions (such as 1.2, 2.0, and 2.1) and include files in various formats, including **CSV**[1][2][3].

### How to access the CSV files:
1.  **Visit the HDX Dataset Page:** You can access the latest version directly via the following links:
    *   [Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 2.1](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria-version-2-1)[3]
    *   [Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 1.2](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria)[4]
2.  **Locate the Administrative Data:** For versions that include population totals for administrative units (such as states and Local Government Areas), look for ZIP files labeled as **"admin"** (e.g., `NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip`)[5][6][7]. These files typically contain:
    *   **CSV spreadsheets** with the population estimates[5][6][7].
    *   Shapefiles for the corresponding administrative boundaries[5][6].

### Dataset Details
*   **Content:** These datasets provide gridded population estimates at a spatial resolution of 3 arc-seconds (approximately 100 m) and include estimates for various age-sex/gender groups[8][2][3].
*   **Usage:** The CSV files are generally constructed to provide subnational P-codes, administrative names, and population totals[8].
*   **Note:** While the gridded data itself is often in GeoTIFF format[8][9], the population summaries for administrative units are provided in CSV format to allow for easier integration into data analysis workflows[5][6][7].

To download the Nigeria Subnational Administrative Boundaries (COD-AB) dataset in XLSX or other formats, you can access the official page on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)[1][2].

### **Direct Access**
You can find the dataset at this link:
**[Nigeria - Subnational Administrative Boundaries | HDX](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ab-nga)**

### **How to Download**
1.  **Navigate to the Dataset Page:** Use the link above to reach the official dataset page[2].
2.  **Locate the "Data and Resources" Section:** On the dataset page, you will see a list of available files under the "Data and Resources" section[3][4].
3.  **Select Your Format:**
    *   **XLSX:** Look for the resource labeled `nga_admin_boundaries.xlsx`[3][4]. Click the **Download** button next to it[5].
    *   **Other Formats:** The page also provides the data in other formats, including **Geodatabase (.gdb.zip)**, **Shapefile (.shp.zip)**, and **GeoJSON**[1][3][2].
4.  **Metadata Export:** If you only need the metadata, you can export it in JSON or CSV format using the links provided in the dataset's metadata section[1].

The data is provided by OCHA Nigeria and is updated periodically to ensure it remains a reliable reference for humanitarian operations[6][7].

You can find various Nigeria population datasets in XLSX and CSV formats on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) platform. Key datasets include:

*   **[Nigeria - Subnational Population Statistics](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ps-nga):** Provides administrative level 0-1 sex and age-disaggregated 2022 projected population statistics. Files are available in **XLSX** (e.g., `nga_admpop_2022.xlsx`) and **CSV** (e.g., `nga_admpop_adm0_2022.csv`, `nga_admpop_adm1_2022.csv`) formats[1][2].
*   **[Nigeria Subnational Population and Housing Data Tables](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/):** Published by the U.S. Census Bureau, this dataset includes tabular data with total population estimates, five-year age groups, sex, and household information, often available in **CSV** formats[3].
*   **[Baseline Population Datasets](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/):** HDX hosts several other population-related datasets for Nigeria, including high-resolution population density maps and demographic estimates[4].

You can search for more specific files directly on the [HDX Nigeria page](https://data.humdata.org/) by filtering for "Nigeria" and selecting "XLSX" or "CSV" under the file format options[5].

{
  "CodeContent": "import os\nimport requests\nimport pandas as pd\n\ndef download_and_extract_population():\n    raw_dir = \"/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw\"\n    os.makedirs(raw_dir, exist_ok=True)\n    \n    url = \"https://data.humdata.org/dataset/a7c3de5e-ff27-4746-99cd-05f2ad9b1066/resource/d3a631f1-d9e1-430c-a10e-ff34be40410d/download/nga_admpop_2022.xlsx\"\n    target_path = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_admpop_2022.xlsx\")\n    \n    print(f\"Downloading population data from {url}...\")\n    response = requests.get(url, stream=True)\n    if response.status_code == 200:\n        with open(target_path, 'wb') as f:\n            for chunk in response.iter_content(chunk_size=8192):\n                f.write(chunk)\n        print(f\"Saved raw dataset to {target_path}\")\n    else:\n        print(f\"Failed to download file. Status code: {response.status_code}\")\n        return\n\n    # Load Excel file to inspect sheets\n    print(\"Reading Excel sheets...\")\n    xls = pd.ExcelFile(target_path)\n    print(f\"Available sheets: {xls.sheet_names}\")\n    \n    # Usually the sheets are named after administrative levels, e.g., adm0, adm1, adm2\n    # Let's find the sheet representing LGA level (adm2)\n    lga_sheet = None\n    for sheet in xls.sheet_names:\n        if \"adm2\" in sheet.lower() or \"lga\" in sheet.lower():\n            lga_sheet = sheet\n            break\n            \n    if not lga_sheet:\n        # Default to the sheet containing \"population\" or the 3rd sheet\n        lga_sheet = xls.sheet_names[2] if len(xls.sheet_names) > 2 else xls.sheet_names[-1]\n    \n    print(f\"Reading LGA data from sheet: {lga_sheet}...\")\n    df = pd.read_excel(target_path, sheet_name=lga_sheet)\n    print(\"Columns found in sheet:\")\n    print(df.columns.tolist())\n    print(\"\\nFirst few rows:\")\n    print(df.head())\n    \n    # Filter for BAY states: Borno, Adamawa, Yobe\n    # Let's search for column name representing the state\n    state_col = None\n    for col in df.columns:\n        if \"adm1\" in col.lower() or \"state\" in col.lower() or \"region\" in col.lower():\n            state_col = col\n            break\n            \n    if not state_col:\n        print(\"Could not find state/admin1 column. Available columns:\", df.columns.tolist())\n        return\n        \n    print(f\"Filtering using column: {state_col}\")\n    \n    bay_states = [\"Adamawa\", \"Borno\", \"Yobe\"]\n    # Filter rows case-insensitively or matching exactly\n    filtered_df = df[df[state_col].astype(str).str.strip().str.title().isin(bay_states)]\n    \n    # Save the filtered results to a CSV in raw folder\n    out_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_bay_lga_population_2022.csv\")\n    filtered_df.to_csv(out_csv, index=False)\n    print(f\"\\nSaved BAY states LGA population data to {out_csv}\")\n    print(f\"Total LGAs extracted: {len(filtered_df)}\")\n    \n    # Display the LGA breakdown\n    print(\"\\n--- BAY States LGA Population Summary ---\")\n    print(filtered_df.to_string())\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    download_and_extract_population()\n",

The search for "Nigeria LGA population statistics csv xlsx site:data.humdata.org" returned the following summary:
You can access Nigeria subnational population statistics, including data suitable for LGA-level linkage, on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) website.

### Recommended Datasets
*   **[Nigeria - Subnational Population Statistics (UNFPA)](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ps-nga):** This is the primary dataset for projected population statistics. It includes:
    *   **File Formats:** Available in **XLSX** and **CSV** formats[1].
    *   **Content:** Provides sex and age disaggregated 2022 population statistics projected from the 2006 Census[1][2]. 
    *   **Utility:** These tables are designed for database or GIS linkage to administrative level 2 (LGA) boundary shapefiles[1][3].
*   **[Nigeria - Subnational Administrative Boundaries](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nigeria-administrative-boundaries):** To map or link population data to LGAs, you will need these boundary files (available in SHP, Geodatabase, and GeoJSON)[4].

### Alternative Sources on HDX
*   **[U.S. Census Bureau Nigeria Data](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nigeria-subnational-population-and-housing-data-tables-with-administrative-boundaries-based-on-national-census-and-surveys):** Contains tabular data with total population for 2006 (census) and 2019 (estimates), including five-year age groups and sex disaggregation[5].
*   **[Risk Assessment Indicators (HeiGIT)](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nigeria-risk-assessment-indicators):** Provides various demographic and population-related indicators (including rural population) aggregated at the Admin 2 (LGA) level[6].

**Tip:** When searching on HDX, look for files labeled as **"P-coded"**; these include the administrative codes (P-codes) necessary to join tabular population data with the corresponding administrative boundary shapefiles[1].

The search for "site:data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ps-nga resource download csv xlsx" returned the following summary:
To download the CSV or XLSX files for the **Nigeria - Subnational Population Statistics** dataset (COD-PS) from the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX), you can access them directly via the official dataset page[1][2].

### **Direct Access**
*   **Dataset URL:** [https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ps-nga](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ps-nga)[1]

### **Available Resources**
Once on the page, look under the **"Data and Resources"** section[1]. You will find the following files available for download:

*   **XLSX (Projected Population Statistics):**
    *   `nga_admpop_2022.xlsx` (Nigeria administrative level 0-1 sex and age disaggregated 2022 projected population statistics)[1][3].
*   **XLSX (Gazetteer):**
    *   `nga_admgz.xlsx` (Gazetteer for administrative levels 0, 1, 2, and 3)[1][3].
*   **CSV (Disaggregated Population Data):**
    *   `nga_admpop_adm0_2022.csv` (Level 0 sex disaggregated data)[1][3].
    *   `nga_admpop_adm1_2022.csv` (Level 1 sex disaggregated data)[3].
*   **Supporting Documentation:**
    *   `nga_codps_qa_2023.pdf` (Technical note explaining the population statistics)[1][3].

### **How to Download**
1.  Navigate to the [dataset link](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/cod-ps-nga)[1].
2.  Scroll down to the **Data and Resources** list[1].
3.  Click on the **"Download"** button or the file name associated with the resource you need[1].

*Note: These datasets are part of the Common Operational Datasets (COD) for Nigeria and are intended to support humanitarian planning and GIS linkage[1][2][4]. Please ensure you review any technical notes or caveats provided in the documentation[1][2].*

The search for "site:data.humdata.org "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria" "LGA" OR "admin" OR "csv"" returned the following summary:
The "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria" datasets on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) provide population totals for administrative units, including states and Local Government Areas (LGAs)[1][2].

These datasets typically include the following formats:
*   **CSV Spreadsheets:** The population estimates are provided in CSV format for easy integration and analysis[1][2].
*   **Shapefiles (SHP):** Administrative boundaries (such as LGAs and Wards) are often included as shapefiles, with population estimates integrated into the attribute tables[1][2].
*   **Grid Data:** The estimates are also available as gridded population data (e.g., GeoTIFF) at a spatial resolution of approximately 100 meters[3][4][5].

You can find these files by searching for "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria" on the [HDX platform](https://data.humdata.org/), where versions such as 1.2, 2.0, and 2.1 are available[1][2][5]. Ensure you check the specific resource files within each dataset for the desired administrative level (e.g., LGA or Ward)[1].

The search for "site:data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria-version-2-1 download csv" returned the following summary:
To access the "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 2.1" dataset, please visit the official Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) page:

**[Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 2.1](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria-version-2-1)**

### Important Information Regarding Data Format
Please note that this dataset consists of **gridded raster data (GeoTIFFs)**[1][2], not CSV files. 
*   **Data Structure:** The data is provided as high-resolution (100m) raster grids[3][1].
*   **Why no CSV:** Because this is gridded spatial data (where population counts are assigned to specific geographic cells), it is typically distributed in raster formats (like .tif) rather than tabular formats (like .csv) to maintain geographic integrity[1].
*   **Available Files:** On the HDX page, you will find ZIP archives containing:
    *   **`NGA_population_v2_1_gridded.zip`**: Contains the main population count rasters[1].
    *   **`NGA_population_v2_1_agegender.zip`**: Contains rasters for specific age-gender groups[2].
    *   **README files**: PDF documentation explaining the methodology and data structure[4].

If you specifically require population data in CSV format (e.g., population by administrative region rather than by grid cell), you may need to use GIS software (such as QGIS or ArcGIS) to extract zonal statistics from these GeoTIFF files or check the HDX platform for separate administrative boundary (cod) datasets that provide tabular population statistics.

Sources:

The "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria" dataset on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) primarily provides high-resolution gridded data (GeoTIFF raster format)[1][2][3].

However, for administrative unit data, you can find the following:

*   **Administrative Data:** In version 1.2 of the dataset, there is a specific file named `NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip`[4]. This file contains population totals for administrative units (States and Local Government Areas) in both **shapefile** format (with estimates in attribute tables) and **.csv spreadsheets**[4].
*   **How to access:** You can locate this by visiting the dataset page on HDX (e.g., searching for the dataset name or version 1.2) and looking under the "Data and Resources" section for the `NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip` file[5][4].

Please note that while the gridded (raster) data is updated to newer versions (such as 2.1), you should check the specific resource list on the HDX dataset page to see if a corresponding CSV for administrative units is available for the version you require[1][3][4].

The search for "site:data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip url download" returned the following summary:
To download the `NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip` file, you can access it through the **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)** platform, which hosts the "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 1.2" dataset[1][2].

### How to Access the File
1.  **Directly via HDX:** Visit the [Humanitarian Dataset page for Nigeria population estimates](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria)[3][1].
2.  **Locate the Resource:** Scroll to the "Data & Resources" section of the page. You will find `NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip` listed among the available files (often grouped with other release content like `NGA_population_v1_2_gridded.zip` and `NGA_population_v1_2_methods.zip`)[1][4][2].
3.  **Download:** Click the download button associated with the file[3].

### Alternative: Programmatic Access (R)
If you are using R, you can use the `wopr` package to download this specific dataset[5][6]:

```R
# Install the wopr package if not already installed
# devtools::install_github('wpgp/wopr')
library(wopr)

# Retrieve the catalogue
catalogue <- getCatalogue()

# Subset for Nigeria (NGA) and version 1.2
selection <- subset(catalogue, country == 'NGA' & category == 'Population' & version == 'v1.2')

# Download the selected files
downloadData(selection)
```
The files will typically be downloaded to your working directory under `./wopr/NGA/population/v1.2/`[5].

**Note:** This dataset includes population totals for administrative units (states and local government areas) and contains shapefiles and CSV spreadsheets[3][7]. Please ensure you review the **Creative Commons Attribution Share-Alike 4.0** license terms provided on the HDX page before use[1][8].

To access the **Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 1.2**, you can visit the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) platform[1].

While specific file download URLs are often managed through the HDX interface, you can locate and download the various components of this dataset directly from the official WorldPop and HDX pages[1][2][3].

### Available Dataset Resources
The release includes several downloadable files[1]:
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_gridded.zip**: Gridded population estimates[1][4].
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip**: Population totals for administrative units[1][2].
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_agesex.zip**: Estimates for individual age-sex groups[1][5].
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_sql.sql**: SQL database with Bayesian posterior predictions[1][3].
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_mastergrid.tif**: Master grid file[1].
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_tiles.zip**: Image tiles for visualization[1][6].
*   **NGA_population_v1_2_methods.zip**: Methods documentation[1].

### Important Note on Newer Versions
Please be aware that newer data, such as **version 2.1** of the "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria," is also available on the Humanitarian Data Exchange[7][8]. Depending on your research needs, you may want to verify if version 2.1 is more appropriate for your work, as it represents updated estimates[8].

The "Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria" datasets on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) include several versions, each with different files available for download. Based on your search for `admin.zip` or `admin` files, here are the relevant resources found across the versions:

### Available Administrative Data Files
*   **Version 1.2:** Contains an administrative level data file named **`NGA_population_v1_2_admin.zip`**[1][2]. This file includes population totals for administrative units (states and local government areas) in Nigeria, with boundaries provided as shapefiles and population estimates in .csv spreadsheets[1][2].
*   **Version 2.0:** Contains an administrative level data file named **`NGA_population_v2_0_admin.zip`**[3][4]. This file similarly provides population totals for administrative units, including summaries for Wards and Local Government Areas (LGAs) with shapefiles and .csv spreadsheets[3][4].

1.  **Version 1.2 Dataset:** [Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 1.2](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria)[5]

To download settlement and population data for Nigeria from the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX), you can access the following datasets directly through their HDX pages[1][2][3]:

    *   **Download Page:** [Nigeria: High Resolution Population Density Maps | HDX](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nigeria-high-resolution-population-density-maps-demographic-estimates)[3]

2.  Scroll down to the **"Data & Resources"** section of the page[4].
3.  Click the **"Download"** button next to the file format (SHP or XLSX) you wish to obtain[1][2][4].

To download population and settlement data for Nigeria from the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX), you can visit the platform directly and use its search and filter tools. There is no single dataset specifically named "nigeria-settlement-population," but several high-quality datasets match this criteria.

### Recommended Datasets for Nigeria
You can find these by searching [data.humdata.org](https://data.humdata.org/) for "Nigeria population":

*   **Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria:** High-resolution (approx. 100m) grid cell population estimates produced by WorldPop/GRID3.
*   **Nigeria: High Resolution Population Density Maps + Demographic Estimates:** Detailed demographic maps including age and gender disaggregation.
*   **Nigeria - Subnational Population Statistics:** Official projections (e.g., 2022) suitable for administrative and GIS linkages.
*   **Populated Places of Nigeria:** Data on named cities, towns, and villages often sourced from OpenStreetMap.

### How to Download
1.  **Visit HDX:** Go to [data.humdata.org](https://data.humdata.org/).
2.  **Search:** Enter "Nigeria population" or "Nigeria settlements" in the search bar.
3.  **Browse & Select:** Review the list of results to find the dataset that best fits your needs (e.g., gridded estimates vs. administrative statistics).
4.  **Download:** Click on the specific dataset. On the dataset page, you will see a list of available files (resources). Click the **"Download"** button next to the file format you require (e.g., CSV, XLSX, GeoTIFF, or Shapefile).

**Tips:**
*   **Registration:** While anyone can view and download public data without an account, registering for a free HDX account provides access to additional features.
*   **Documentation:** Always check the "Data Description" or "Readme" files provided within the dataset to understand the methodology, reference years, and limitations of the data.

The search for ""wopr.worldpop.org" NGA population" returned the following summary:
The **WorldPop Open Population Repository (WOPR)** (available at [wopr.worldpop.org](https://wopr.worldpop.org)) is an online platform that provides access to high-resolution, gridded population estimates for various countries, including **Nigeria (NGA)**[1][2][3].

### Key Information regarding "NGA" and WOPR:

*   **What it is:** The WOPR provides bespoke, "bottom-up" modelled population estimates[1][3]. Unlike standard top-down models, these are often co-developed with national governments and partners (such as the GRID3 project) to provide more accurate data, especially in areas where census data may be outdated or incomplete[1][3][4].
*   **Nigeria Data (NGA):** The repository hosts specific datasets for Nigeria, including:
    *   **Gridded Population Estimates:** High-resolution maps of population distribution[1][5].
    *   **Demographic Breakdowns:** Data categorized by age and sex[1].
    *   **Supporting Data:** Associated datasets such as building footprints and administrative boundaries used in the modeling process[1][3].
*   **How to access it:**
    *   **Web Repository:** You can browse the files directly at [wopr.worldpop.org/?NGA](https://wopr.worldpop.org/?NGA/Population)[6][3].
    *   **woprVision:** An interactive web-based interface ([woprVision](https://apps.worldpop.org/woprVision/)) is available to explore and visualize these population estimates[7][8].
    *   **R Package:** An R package named `wopr` is available on GitHub, allowing researchers to programmatically query the repository, retrieve population estimates for specific geographic areas, and access uncertainty metrics[9][7][10].

If you are looking for specific versions of the Nigeria data (e.g., v1.2, v2.0), they can be found within the repository's directory structure under the "Population" or "Buildings" folders for "NGA"[1][11][12].

### Key Information Regarding These Identifiers:
*   **Context:** These types of identifiers are frequently found in datasets hosted on humanitarian or geographic data platforms, such as the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)[2].
*   **Data Content:** They typically contain sex- and age-disaggregated population projections for Nigeria[3][2]. For example, a file labeled `nga_admpop_adm2_2022` would generally contain projected population data for the year 2022, structured by administrative boundaries that can be linked to GIS shapefiles[2].
*   **Administrative Levels:**
    *   **Adm0:** National level[3][2].
    *   **Adm1:** State level[1][2].
    *   **Adm2:** Local Government Area (LGA) level[1][2].
*   **Source:** Such data is often derived from projections based on census data (e.g., the 2006 Census) and published by organizations like the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) or used by international humanitarian agencies to support planning and development efforts[4][2].

If you are looking for these specific files, it is recommended to search the **[Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)](https://data.humdata.org/)** website, specifically looking for Nigeria subnational population datasets, as they maintain the standard naming conventions for these administrative population files[2].

  "CodeContent": "import os\nimport zipfile\nimport requests\nimport pandas as pd\nimport glob\n\ndef download_and_extract_v2_0():\n    raw_dir = \"/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw\"\n    os.makedirs(raw_dir, exist_ok=True)\n    \n    url = \"https://wopr.worldpop.org/repo/wopr/NGA/population/v2.0/NGA_population_v2_0_admin.zip\"\n    target_zip = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"NGA_population_v2_0_admin.zip\")\n    extract_dir = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"NGA_population_v2_0_admin\")\n    \n    print(f\"Downloading population data from {url}...\")\n    response = requests.get(url, stream=True)\n    if response.status_code == 200:\n        with open(target_zip, 'wb') as f:\n            for chunk in response.iter_content(chunk_size=8192):\n                f.write(chunk)\n        print(f\"Saved raw zip to {target_zip}\")\n    else:\n        print(f\"Failed to download file. Status code: {response.status_code}\")\n        # Try fallbacks or check URL\n        return\n\n    print(\"Extracting zip file...\")\n    with zipfile.ZipFile(target_zip, 'r') as zip_ref:\n        zip_ref.extractall(extract_dir)\n    print(f\"Extracted to {extract_dir}\")\n    \n    # List all files extracted\n    extracted_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(extract_dir, \"**/*\"), recursive=True)\n    print(\"Extracted files:\")\n    for f in extracted_files:\n        if os.path.isfile(f):\n            print(f\" - {os.path.relpath(f, raw_dir)}\")\n\n    # Let's search for CSV files containing population data\n    csv_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(extract_dir, \"**/*.csv\"), recursive=True)\n    if not csv_files:\n        print(\"No CSV files found in the extracted archive.\")\n        return\n        \n    print(f\"Found CSV files: {csv_files}\")\n    \n    # Let's look for the LGA population table\n    lga_csv = None\n    for csv_file in csv_files:\n        if \"lga\" in os.path.basename(csv_file).lower() or \"admin2\" in os.path.basename(csv_file).lower():\n            lga_csv = csv_file\n            break\n            \n    if not lga_csv:\n        # Fallback to the first CSV\n        lga_csv = csv_files[0]\n        \n    print(f\"Reading LGA population data from {lga_csv}...\")\n    df = pd.read_csv(lga_csv)\n    print(\"Columns found in CSV:\")\n    print(df.columns.tolist())\n    print(\"\\nFirst few rows:\")\n    print(df.head())\n    \n    # Filter for BAY states: Borno, Adamawa, Yobe\n    state_col = None\n    for col in df.columns:\n        if \"state\" in col.lower() or \"adm1\" in col.lower() or \"region\" in col.lower():\n            state_col = col\n            break\n            \n    if not state_col:\n        print(\"Could not find state/admin1 column in CSV. Available columns:\", df.columns.tolist())\n        return\n        \n    print(f\"Filtering using column: {state_col}\")\n    bay_states = [\"Adamawa\", \"Borno\", \"Yobe\"]\n    filtered_df = df[df[state_col].astype(str).str.strip().str.title().isin(bay_states)]\n    \n    # Save the filtered results to a CSV in raw folder\n    out_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv\")\n    filtered_df.to_csv(out_csv, index=False)\n    print(f\"\\nSaved BAY states LGA population data to {out_csv}\")\n    print(f\"Total LGAs extracted: {len(filtered_df)}\")\n    \n    # Display the LGA breakdown\n    print(\"\\n--- BAY States LGA Population Summary ---\")\n    print(filtered_df.to_string())\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    download_and_extract_v2_0()\n",

The search for "site:wopr.worldpop.org NGA" returned the following summary:
The URL **wopr.worldpop.org** refers to the **WorldPop Open Population Repository (WOPR)**[1][2]. It is a platform maintained by the WorldPop research group at the University of Southampton that provides access to high-resolution, "bottom-up" modelled gridded population datasets[3][4][5].

### Accessing Nigeria (NGA) Data
The repository hosts specific datasets for Nigeria (using the ISO code **NGA**)[1], which are often produced in collaboration with initiatives like GRID3 (Geo-Referenced Infrastructure and Demographic Data for Development)[3][6].

*   **Data Content:** The WOPR site provides gridded population estimates (typically at 100m resolution)[7][6], often including associated uncertainty metrics[7][8][9], age-sex disaggregated data[3][10], and building footprint datasets[11][12].
*   **How to Access:**
    *   **Direct Web Access:** You can browse and download datasets directly by navigating the repository structure (e.g., `https://wopr.worldpop.org/?NGA/Population`)[13][14][4].
    *   **WoprVision:** This is an interactive web map application that allows users to explore and query population estimates for specific locations[15][16][2]. It is available at [apps.worldpop.org/woprVision](https://apps.worldpop.org/woprVision)[16].
    *   **R Package:** Users can also interface with the repository using the `wopr` R package, which allows for programmatic downloading of data and spatial querying[1][16][9]. You can install it from GitHub (`wpgp/wopr`)[16].

### Context on "Bottom-Up" Data
WorldPop differentiates between "top-down" (disaggregating census totals) and "bottom-up" (using microcensus survey data to model population) approaches[17][4][18]. The datasets for Nigeria found on WOPR are typically the result of **bottom-up** modelling[3][7][4], which is particularly useful in settings where census data may be outdated, incomplete, or inaccessible[19][4][5].

The search for "site:data.humdata.org/dataset/ bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria-version-2-0" returned the following summary:
The **Bottom-up gridded population estimates for Nigeria, version 2.0**, available on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX), provides high-resolution (approximately 100 m grid cells) population estimates for Nigeria[1][2][3].

### Key Details
*   **Data Content:** The dataset includes gridded population totals, uncertainty estimates, and breakdowns for various age-sex groups[4][1][2]. It also provides population summaries for administrative units, such as states and Local Government Areas (LGAs)[5].
*   **Methodology:** Version 2.0 is an update to version 1.2, utilizing more recent and detailed settlement information and different regional boundary definitions[2][6][3]. The estimates are model-based, representing population data around 2019—the period when the satellite imagery used for generating building footprints was processed[2][6].
*   **Source:** Produced by the **WorldPop Research Group** at the University of Southampton in collaboration with the National Population Commission of Nigeria, as part of the **GRID3** (Geo-Referenced Infrastructure and Demographic Data for Development) programme[1][2].

### Available Files
The dataset typically includes:
*   **Geotiff rasters:** Containing population estimates and uncertainty metrics[4].
*   **Admin unit summaries:** Zip files containing population totals for states and LGAs[5][6].
*   **SQL database:** Samples from Bayesian posterior predictions[7][6].
*   **Documentation:** A README file explaining the methodology, source data, and limitations[8][6].

You can access the files directly through the [HDX dataset page](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/bottom-up-gridded-population-estimates-for-nigeria-version-2-0). Please note that a newer **version 2.1** is also available, which provides a correction to the settlement map[1].

I have successfully downloaded, extracted, and filtered the latest high-resolution bottom-up population statistics for Nigeria at the Local Government Area (LGA) level.

### 1. Downloaded Files

*   **Original Zip Archive:** [NGA_population_v2_0_admin.zip](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/NGA_population_v2_0_admin.zip) (GRID3/WorldPop Admin 0/1/2/Ward baseline estimates).
*   **Extracted Directory:** [NGA_population_v2_0_admin/](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/NGA_population_v2_0_admin/) (Contains Shapefiles and CSVs for all admin levels in Nigeria).
*   **Filtered BAY LGA Population Data:** [nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv) (A clean CSV containing LGA population metrics for Borno, Adamawa, and Yobe states).

### Summary of Work Done
*   Created the download script [download_population.py](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/scripts/download_population.py) to fetch the GRID3/WorldPop bottom-up administrative population estimates for Nigeria (version 2.0).
*   Executed the script to download the zip file and extract all level-0, level-1, level-2, and Ward-level population CSVs and GIS shapefiles to `data/raw/NGA_population_v2_0_admin/`.

[2026-06-26T22:49:04Z] [STEP 130] AGENT ACTION:
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Response:
The file [nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv) contains bottom-up gridded population model estimates for the **65 Local Government Areas (LGAs)** that make up the Borno, Adamawa, and Yobe (BAY) states in northeastern Nigeria.

Below is an overview of the dataset schema, summary statistics, and regional breakdown:

---

### 1. Data Schema & Columns
The dataset contains **11 columns** representing administrative metadata and Bayesian statistical distribution metrics:

| Column Name | Data Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `state` | Text | The name of the state (either `Adamawa`, `Borno`, or `Yobe`). |
| `local` | Text | The name of the Local Government Area (LGA). |
| `mean` | Decimal | The modeled mean population estimate of the LGA. |
| `sd` | Decimal | The standard deviation of the population estimate. |
| `q025` | Decimal | The 2.5th percentile (lower bound of the 95% uncertainty interval). |
| `q05` | Decimal | The 5th percentile. |
| `q25` | Decimal | The 25th percentile (lower quartile). |
| `q50` | Decimal | The 50th percentile (median population estimate). |
| `q75` | Decimal | The 75th percentile (upper quartile). |
| `q95` | Decimal | The 95th percentile. |
| `q975` | Decimal | The 97.5th percentile (upper bound of the 95% uncertainty interval). |

---

### 2. State-Level Summary Statistics
Across the entire dataset, the total estimated population for the BAY region is **15,998,670.77** (Mean) or **15,879,745.50** (Median).

#### **Adamawa State** (21 LGAs)
*   **Total Population (Mean):** 5,066,720.17
*   **Average LGA Population:** 241,272.39
*   **Smallest LGA:** Numan (93,642.04)
*   **Largest LGA:** Fufore (460,889.36)

#### **Borno State** (27 LGAs)
*   **Total Population (Mean):** 6,026,749.06
*   **Average LGA Population:** 223,212.93
*   **Smallest LGA:** Kala/Balge (74,057.77)
*   **Largest LGA:** Maiduguri (846,657.06)

#### **Yobe State** (17 LGAs)
*   **Total Population (Mean):** 4,905,201.54
*   **Average LGA Population:** 288,541.27
*   **Smallest LGA:** Karasuwa (163,835.49)
*   **Largest LGA:** Potiskum (524,193.58)

---

### Summary of Work Done
*   Inspected the schema of the CSV file [nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv](file:///workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw/nga_bay_lga_population_v2.csv).
*   Created a script to analyze the record counts, column descriptions, and compute summary aggregates (e.g. sums, means, minimums, and maximums) for each of the three states.
*   Documented the Bayesian probability interval fields (`q025` to `q975`) representing the uncertainty modeling of the source GRID3 bottom-up estimates.

The search for "Nigeria schools dataset Borno Adamawa Yobe HDX" returned the following summary:
You can access datasets related to schools in Borno, Adamawa, and Yobe (BAY states) through the **Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)** platform, which is managed by OCHA[1][2].

### Relevant Datasets and Resources on HDX
*   **Nigeria Education Facilities:** You can find geospatial data (such as SHP, GeoJSON, and KML formats) representing education facilities across Nigeria, including the North East, via the [HDX Nigeria Education Facilities](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/nigeria-education-facilities) page[3][4][5]. 
*   **Historical School Lists:** There are specific historical datasets, such as the *North East Nigeria – Borno, Yobe and Adamawa States School List (as of June 2019)*, which provide detailed breakdowns of school names, types (e.g., Islamiyya, Nomadic), levels, and operational status[6].
*   **Other Relevant Data:** HDX also hosts broader humanitarian data for the BAY states, including displacement tracking[7], humanitarian access constraints[8], and general education indicators for Nigeria[9][10].

### Alternative Data Sources
If you require more current or granular information for educational planning:
*   **DHIS2-EMIS Projects:** UNICEF and partners have been working to enhance education data management in Nigeria, including in Borno, Adamawa, and Yobe, through digital Education Management Information Systems (EMIS)[11].
*   **State-Level EMIS:** Some states have their own EMIS portals (e.g., Adamawa and Yobe) that provide data on public and private schools, school locations, and other education statistics[12].
*   **National Education Reports:** The *Nigeria Digest of Education Statistics* (produced by the Federal Ministry of Education) is a primary source for comprehensive, evidence-based education data[13].

**How to access these on HDX:**
1.  Visit the [Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)](https://data.humdata.org/) website[1].
2.  Search for "Nigeria education" or "Borno Adamawa Yobe schools" in the search bar[9][4].
3.  Use the filter options on the left side of the screen to refine by "Nigeria" or specific tags like "education" or "facilities"[3].

The dataset you are looking for is titled **"North East Nigeria – Borno, Yobe and Adamawa States School List as of June 2019"** and is hosted on the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) platform[1][2].

### Dataset Information
*   **Title:** North East Nigeria – Borno, Yobe and Adamawa States School List as of June 2019[2]
*   **Source/Contributor:** iMMAP Inc.[2]
*   **Description:** This dataset provides a comprehensive list of schools at the Local Government Area (LGA) and Ward level for the three crisis-affected states (Borno, Yobe, and Adamawa)[1][3].
*   **Data Details:** It includes information such as the school name, type (e.g., Regular, Islamiyya, Nomadic, Special Needs, Vocational Training Center), education level (e.g., Pre-Primary, Primary, Junior Secondary), and operational status (open or closed)[3][2].

### How to Download
You can access and download the CSV file directly from the Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX) website at the following link:

**[Download Link: North East Nigeria – Borno, Yobe and Adamawa States School List](https://data.humdata.org/dataset/north-east-nigeria-borno-yobe-and-adamawa-states-school-list-as-of-june-2019)**[2]

*Note: On the page, look for the "Data and Resources" section where you will find the `NGA_North_East_Nigeria_Education_Sector_School_List_June 2019.zip` file available for download[2].*

  "CodeContent": "import os\nimport zipfile\nimport requests\nimport geopandas as gpd\nimport pandas as pd\nimport glob\n\ndef download_and_process_shp():\n    raw_dir = \"/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw\"\n    os.makedirs(raw_dir, exist_ok=True)\n    \n    url = \"https://data.humdata.org/dataset/ec228c18-8edc-4f3c-94c9-a6b946af7229/resource/1a064a21-ffcf-4fb8-a0a6-5cf811d94664/download/nga_education.zip\"\n    target_zip = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"NGA_Education.zip\")\n    extract_dir = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"NGA_Education\")\n    \n    print(f\"Downloading ZIP dataset from {url}...\")\n    response = requests.get(url, stream=True)\n    if response.status_code == 200:\n        with open(target_zip, 'wb') as f:\n            for chunk in response.iter_content(chunk_size=8192):\n                f.write(chunk)\n        print(f\"Saved raw ZIP to {target_zip}\")\n    else:\n        print(f\"Failed to download file. Status code: {response.status_code}\")\n        return\n\n    print(\"Extracting ZIP file...\")\n    with zipfile.ZipFile(target_zip, 'r') as zip_ref:\n        zip_ref.extractall(extract_dir)\n    print(f\"Extracted to {extract_dir}\")\n    \n    # List files\n    shp_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(extract_dir, \"**/*.shp\"), recursive=True)\n    print(f\"Found Shapefiles: {shp_files}\")\n    \n    if not shp_files:\n        print(\"No shapefile found!\")\n        return\n        \n    shp_path = shp_files[0]\n    print(f\"Reading shapefile: {shp_path}...\")\n    gdf = gpd.read_file(shp_path)\n    print(\"Columns in shapefile:\")\n    print(gdf.columns.tolist())\n    print(f\"Shape: {gdf.shape}\")\n    print(\"First 3 rows:\")\n    print(gdf.head(3))\n    \n    # Filter for BAY states: state_code AD, BR, YO\n    print(\"Filtering for BAY states (AD, BR, YO)...\")\n    bay_codes = [\"AD\", \"BR\", \"YO\"]\n    filtered_gdf = gdf[gdf[\"state_code\"].isin(bay_codes)].copy()\n    print(f\"Filtered shape: {filtered_gdf.shape}\")\n    \n    if filtered_gdf.empty:\n        print(\"No records found for BAY states!\")\n        return\n        \n    # Extract coordinates from geometry\n    # Check coordinate reference system (CRS) - convert to EPSG:4326 if not already, to get latitude/longitude\n    print(f\"Current CRS: {filtered_gdf.crs}\")\n    if filtered_gdf.crs != \"EPSG:4326\":\n        print(\"Re-projecting to EPSG:4326 (WGS 84)...\")\n        filtered_gdf = filtered_gdf.to_crs(\"EPSG:4326\")\n        \n    # Extract longitude and latitude\n    filtered_gdf[\"longitude\"] = filtered_gdf.geometry.x\n    filtered_gdf[\"latitude\"] = filtered_gdf.geometry.y\n    \n    # Check if there is a status column\n    status_col = None\n    for col in filtered_gdf.columns:\n        if \"status\" in col.lower() or \"operational\" in col.lower():\n            status_col = col\n            break\n            \n    # Save the output CSV with names, status, and coordinates\n    # Let's map state codes to full state names for clarity\n    state_mapping = {\"AD\": \"Adamawa\", \"BR\": \"Borno\", \"YO\": \"Yobe\"}\n    filtered_gdf[\"state_name\"] = filtered_gdf[\"state_code\"].map(state_mapping)\n    \n    # Select columns of interest\n    cols_to_save = [\"state_name\", \"name\", \"category\", \"subtype\", \"management\", \"education\", \"latitude\", \"longitude\"]\n    if status_col:\n        cols_to_save.append(status_col)\n        \n    # We will also keep other columns if they are useful\n    out_df = pd.DataFrame(filtered_gdf)\n    out_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_bay_schools.csv\")\n    out_df.to_csv(out_csv, index=False)\n    \n    print(f\"Saved BAY states schools with coordinates to {out_csv}\")\n    print(f\"Columns saved: {out_df.columns.tolist()}\")\n    print(f\"Total schools saved: {len(out_df)}\")\n    \n    # Print status values\n    if status_col:\n        print(\"\\nStatus distribution:\")\n        print(out_df[status_col].value_counts(dropna=False))\n    else:\n        print(\"\\nNo status column found in shapefile columns!\")\n        \nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    download_and_process_shp()\n",

  "CodeContent": "import pandas as pd\nimport re\nimport os\n\ndef normalize_name(name):\n    if not isinstance(name, str):\n        return \"\"\n    # Lowercase\n    name = name.lower()\n    # Remove punctuation and special chars\n    name = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9\\s]', ' ', name)\n    # Remove extra spaces\n    name = ' '.join(name.split())\n    # Remove common words to aid matching\n    common_words = [\n        \"primary\", \"secondary\", \"school\", \"schools\", \"islamiyya\", \"nomadic\", \n        \"junior\", \"senior\", \"comprehensive\", \"government\", \"ltd\", \"gss\", \"gps\", \n        \"jss\", \"pri\", \"sec\", \"sch\", \"academic\", \"academy\"\n    ]\n    words = name.split()\n    filtered_words = [w for w in words if w not in common_words]\n    return ' '.join(filtered_words) if filtered_words else name\n\ndef merge_datasets():\n    raw_dir = \"/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw\"\n    immap_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_schools_june_2019/nga_north_east_education_sector_school_list_19062019.csv\")\n    grid3_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_bay_schools.csv\")\n    \n    if not os.path.exists(immap_csv) or not os.path.exists(grid3_csv):\n        print(\"Missing input files. Remove empty norm names\n    df_immap = df_immap[df_immap[\"norm_name\"] != \"\"]\n    df_grid3 = df_grid3[df_grid3[\"norm_name\"] != \"\"]\n    \n    # Merge exact match on state and normalized name\n    print(\"Performing exact match on state and normalized name...\")\n    merged_df = pd.merge(\n        df_grid3, \n        df_immap[[\"state_clean\", \"norm_name\", \"School Status\", \"School Level\", \"School Type\", \"School Name\"]], \n        on=[\"state_clean\", \"norm_name\"],\n        how=\"inner\"\n    )\n    \n    print(f\"Matched schools count: {len(merged_df)}\")\n    \n    #   "CodeContent": "import os\nimport geopandas as gpd\nimport pandas as pd\nimport re\n\ndef normalize_name(name):\n    if not isinstance(name, str):\n        return \"\"\n    name = name.lower()\n    name = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9\\s]', ' ', name)\n    name = ' '.join(name.split())\n    common_words = [\n        \"primary\", \"secondary\", \"school\", \"schools\", \"islamiyya\", \"nomadic\", \n        \"junior\", \"senior\", \"comprehensive\", \"government\", \"ltd\", \"gss\", \"gps\", \n        \"jss\", \"pri\", \"sec\", \"sch\", \"academic\", \"academy\"\n    ]\n    words = name.split()\n    filtered_words = [w for w in words if w not in common_words]\n    return ' '.join(filtered_words) if filtered_words else name\n\ndef normalize_lga(name):\n    if not isinstance(name, str):\n        return \"\"\n    name = name.lower()\n    # Remove characters like hyphens or slashes to make matching robust\n    name = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]', '', name)\n    return name\n\ndef merge_schools_spatial():\n    raw_dir = \"/workspaces/NigeriaBAY/data/raw\"\n    immap_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_schools_june_2019/nga_north_east_education_sector_school_list_19062019.csv\")\n    schools_shp = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"NGA_Education/NGA_Education.shp\")\n    lga_shp = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_shp/nga_admin2.shp\")\n    \n    if not all(os.path.exists(f) for f in [immap_csv, schools_shp, lga_shp]):\n        print(\"Missing required files for spatial join.\")\n        return\n        \n    print(\"Loading datasets...\")\n    df_immap = pd.read_csv(immap_csv, encoding='latin1')\n    gdf_schools = gpd.read_file(schools_shp)\n    gdf_lgas = gpd.read_file(lga_shp)\n    \n    print(f\"Schools count in GRID3: {len(gdf_schools)}\")\n    print(f\"LGA polygons count: {len(gdf_lgas)}\")\n    \n    # Filter schools for BAY states (AD, BR, YO)\n    bay_codes = [\"AD\", \"BR\", \"YO\"]\n    gdf_schools = gdf_schools[gdf_schools[\"state_code\"].isin(bay_codes)].copy()\n    print(f\"Filtered schools in BAY states: {len(gdf_schools)}\")\n    \n    # Ensure matching CRS for spatial join\n    print(\"Aligning CRS...\")\n    if gdf_schools.crs != gdf_lgas.crs:\n        print(f\"Re-projecting schools from {gdf_schools.crs} to {gdf_lgas.crs}...\")\n        gdf_schools = gdf_schools.to_crs(gdf_lgas.crs)\n        \n    # Perform spatial join to attach LGA name to school points\n    print(\"Performing spatial join...\")\n    # gdf_lgas has LGA names (usually columns like 'adm2_name' or 'adm2_en')\n    print(\"LGA columns:\", gdf_lgas.columns.tolist())\n    \n    # Look for LGA name column in shapefile\n    lga_name_col = None\n    for col in gdf_lgas.columns:\n        if col.lower() in [\"adm2_name\", \"adm2_en\", \"lga_name\", \"local\"]:\n            lga_name_col = col\n            break\n    if not lga_name_col:\n        lga_name_col = gdf_lgas.columns[0]\n        \n    print(f\"Using LGA name column from shapefile: {lga_name_col}\")\n    \n    # Do spatial join\n    schools_joined = gpd.sjoin(gdf_schools, gdf_lgas[[lga_name_col, \"geometry\"]], how=\"left\", predicate=\"intersects\")\n    print(f\"Schools after spatial join: {len(schools_joined)}\")\n    \n    # Extract coordinates\n    schools_joined = schools_joined.to_crs(\"EPSG:4326\")\n    schools_joined[\"longitude\"] = schools_joined.geometry.x\n    schools_joined[\"latitude\"] = schools_joined.geometry.y\n    \n    # Prepare for matching\n    df_immap[\"state_clean\"] = df_immap[\"State Name\"].astype(str).str.strip().str.title()\n    state_mapping = {\"AD\": \"Adamawa\", \"BR\": \"Borno\", \"YO\": \"Yobe\"}\n    schools_joined[\"state_clean\"] = schools_joined[\"state_code\"].map(state_mapping)\n    \n    # Normalize LGA names in both datasets\n    df_immap[\"lga_norm\"] = df_immap[\"LGA Name\"].apply(normalize_lga)\n    schools_joined[\"lga_norm\"] = schools_joined[lga_name_col].apply(normalize_lga)\n    \n    # Normalize school names\n    df_immap[\"norm_name\"] = df_immap[\"School Name\"].apply(normalize_name)\n    schools_joined[\"norm_name\"] = schools_joined[\"name\"].apply(normalize_name)\n    \n    # Drop rows with empty names\n    df_immap = df_immap[df_immap[\"norm_name\"] != \"\"]\n    schools_joined = schools_joined[schools_joined[\"norm_name\"] != \"\"]\n    \n    # Perform inner merge on state, LGA, and normalized school name\n    print(\"Merging on state, LGA, and normalized school name...\")\n    merged_df = pd.merge(\n        schools_joined,\n        df_immap[[\"state_clean\", \"lga_norm\", \"norm_name\", \"School Status\", \"School Level\", \"School Type\", \"School Name\"]],\n        on=[\"state_clean\", \"lga_norm\", \"norm_name\"],\n        how=\"inner\"\n    )\n    \n    print(f\"Merged schools count (with duplicates): {len(merged_df)}\")\n    merged_df = merged_df.drop_duplicates(subset=[\"id\"])\n    print(f\"Unique matched schools count: {len(merged_df)}\")\n    \n    # Let's save this as the final clean dataset!\n    # Rename columns for clarity\n    merged_df = merged_df.rename(columns={\n        \"name\": \"school_name_grid3\",\n        \"School Name\": \"school_name_immap\",\n        lga_name_col: \"lga_name\",\n        \"School Status\": \"status\",\n        \"School Level\": \"education_level\",\n        \"School Type\": \"school_type\"\n    })\n    \n    cols_to_save = [\n        \"id\", \"state_clean\", \"lga_name\", \"school_name_grid3\", \"school_name_immap\",\n        \"status\", \"education_level\", \"school_type\", \"latitude\", \"longitude\",\n        \"category\", \"subtype\", \"management\"\n    ]\n    \n    # Filter columns that exist\n    cols_to_save = [c for c in cols_to_save if c in merged_df.columns]\n    \n    out_df = pd.DataFrame(merged_df[cols_to_save])\n    # Rename state_clean to state\n    out_df = out_df.rename(columns={\"state_clean\": \"state\"})\n    \n    out_csv = os.path.join(raw_dir, \"nga_bay_schools_with_status_spatial.csv\")\n    out_df.to_csv(out_csv, index=False)\n    print(f\"Saved spatially matched schools with status and coordinates to {out_csv}\")\n    \n    print(\"\\nMatched Status distribution:\")\n    print(out_df[\"status\"].value_counts(dropna=False))\n    \nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    merge_schools_spatial()\n",
